1: Wikipedia uses 'Kyiv' (since 2019), but retains 'Kiev' in derivatives: Chicken Kiev, Kievan Rus, Grand Prince of Kiev. AP, BBC, UN all adopted 'Kyiv'. Moscow Patriarchate (mospat.ru) exclusively uses 'Kiev'.
2: Wikipedia/Britannica use 'Kharkiv'. 2022 Battle of Kharkiv accelerated adoption in war coverage.
3: Wikipedia switched to 'Odesa' post-2022. UN GEGN approved Ukrainian form in 2012. Cultural brand (Odessa Steps, Odessa Tales) creates self-reinforcing resistance. 13 US namesakes add contamination.
4: Wikipedia/Britannica use 'Lviv'. Highest city adoption — Polish/Habsburg heritage means Russian 'Lvov' had weak cultural roots.
5: Wikipedia uses 'Zaporizhzhia'. Nuclear plant crisis made it globally known. High adoption despite extreme orthographic difficulty (triple zh).
6: Wikipedia uses 'Mykolaiv'. Extreme orthographic distance (Nikolaev→Mykolaiv) slows adoption even with institutional switch.
7: Wikipedia uses 'Dnipro'. Officially renamed under 2016 decommunization. Shorter Ukrainian form ('Dnipro' vs 'Dnepropetrovsk') accelerated adoption.
8: Wikipedia uses 'Vinnytsia'. Mid-tier adoption tracking general geographic pattern.
9: Wikipedia uses 'Rivne'. Western Ukrainian cities generally show higher adoption.
10: Wikipedia retains 'Chernobyl' despite Talk page debates. UN UNGA passed resolution adopting 'Chornobyl' in Dec 2025 (97 votes for). IAEA/Library of Congress now use 'Chornobyl'. Disaster brand resists — Chernobyl Children International kept their name.
11: Wikipedia uses 'Luhansk'. Even under occupation, Western media adopted Ukrainian form — the occupation itself drove coverage.
15: Wikipedia uses 'Dnieper'. Neither Russian nor Ukrainian — an older Western European convention with independent anglicization.
16: Wikipedia uses 'Dniester'. Runs through both Ukraine and Moldova — no Moldovan campaign equivalent creates default persistence.
17: Wikipedia/Britannica use 'Donbas'. Double-s 'Donbass' signals Russian claim to the region.
19: Highest adoption in dataset (95.1%) but tiny corpus. Dominated by official/institutional sources.
20: Small corpus, high institutional adoption but minimal general usage.
21: Wikipedia uses 'Chicken Kiev' (move to 'Kyiv' rejected). Sainsbury's/Birds Eye switched; Aldi/Iceland kept 'Kiev'. Real-world packaging experiment persists into 2026.
22: Kyiv cake tracks below Chicken Kiev — less commercial visibility.
23: Wikipedia/Merriam-Webster/Britannica use 'borscht'. UNESCO inscribed 'Culture of Ukrainian borscht cooking' (2022). Yiddish-mediated spelling entered English independently of Russian/Ukrainian.
24: Wikipedia uses 'Kyiv Pechersk Lavra'. UNESCO World Heritage site listing uses Ukrainian form.
25: Wikipedia uses 'Saint Sophia's Cathedral, Kyiv'. UNESCO listing uses Ukrainian form.
26: Tracks Chernobyl's resistance — the Exclusion Zone inherits the disaster brand.
28: Wikipedia uses 'Taras Shevchenko National University of Kyiv'. Ukrainian universities successfully rebranded.
29: Wikipedia uses 'Karazin Kharkiv National University'. Lower than Kyiv universities.
30: Wikipedia uses 'Igor Sikorsky Kyiv Institute of Technology' — 'Igor' (Russian) + 'Kyiv' (Ukrainian) in same name. Internal contradiction.
31: Moscow Patriarchate uses 'Kiev Patriarchate'. 2019 autocephaly created OCU, displacing both labels.
32: Wikipedia/UEFA use 'Dynamo Kyiv'. UEFA's institutional switch was authoritative — sports media followed.
34: Cultural exports like ballet troupes adopted Ukrainian spelling in touring names.
35: Wikipedia uses 'Kievan Rus''. Russia invokes it for civilizational claims. 'Kievan' as adjective has independent usage.
38: Wikipedia uses 'Chernihiv'. Early 2022 bombardment drove Ukrainian-form coverage.
39: Wikipedia uses 'Chernivtsi'. Former Czernowitz — cultural distinctiveness aids adoption.
40: Wikipedia uses 'Zhytomyr'. Tracking general geographic pattern.
41: Wikipedia uses 'Cherkasy'. Minimal orthographic change (drop one 's') made adoption easy.
43: Wikipedia uses 'Kremenchuk'. 2022 shopping mall missile strike drove Ukrainian-form coverage.
44: Wikipedia uses 'Kropyvnytskyi'. Wholesale decommunization rename (not just transliteration). Oblast still constitutionally 'Kirovohrad'.
45: Wikipedia uses 'Ternopil'. Western Ukrainian city with strong adoption.
46: Wikipedia uses 'Varenyky' (Ukrainian form). At 50%, exact midpoint — institutional switched but broader food world split.
48: Wikipedia uses 'Hopak'. G-to-H substitution succeeding in cultural contexts too.
52: Wikipedia/UEFA use 'Metalist Kharkiv'. Highest sports pair — mirrors Kharkiv's geographic adoption.
54: Wikipedia uses 'Babi Yar'. US Holocaust Memorial uses 'Babyn Yar (Babi Yar)'. Decades of Holocaust literature cement Russian form.
55: Eisenstein's 1925 'Battleship Potemkin' permanently branded these stairs with Russian-era spelling.
56: Motherland Monument tracks Kiev/Kyiv geographic adoption.
57: Wikipedia uses 'Lviv Polytechnic'. Tracks city's high adoption.
58: Lowest institutional adoption (32.3%). Inherits Odessa's cultural entrenchment.
60: Wikipedia/BoxRec/ESPN use 'Oleksandr Usyk'. Active career drives real-time adoption success.
61: Wikipedia uses 'Volodymyr Zelenskyy' (Ukrainian first name + double-y). Double transliteration battleground. Academic literature still favors single-y 'Zelensky'.
62: Wikipedia/UEFA/FIFA use 'Andriy Shevchenko'. Governing body databases cemented Ukrainian form.
70: Wikipedia uses 'Vladimir the Great'. Putin cited shared baptism of Kievan Rus in 2021 essay. Near-zero adoption means English sources implicitly validate Russia's framing.
71: Wikipedia uses 'Grand Prince of Kiev'. Historical titles resist even more than city names.
72: Wikipedia uses 'Bakhmut'. Russia's insistence on 'Artemovsk' was deliberate erasure. 90.2% adoption proves warfare accelerates adoption faster than diplomacy.
80: Wikipedia uses 'Nikolai Gogol'. Google Arts & Culture has Ukrainian-curated 'Mykola Hohol' page. Prospect Magazine: 'Stolen identity: how Nikolai Gogol usurped Mykola Hohol'.
82: Wikipedia uses 'Feodosia' (neither Russian nor Ukrainian). Greek origin adds third etymological layer. Crimean occupation freezes adoption.
83: Wikipedia uses 'Olga of Kiev'. Religious sources use Russian form; Ukrainian nationalist sources use 'Olha of Kyiv'. Split at 50%.
84: Wikipedia uses 'Igor Sikorsky'. Corporate brand (Sikorsky Aircraft), airport name, century of aviation history all lock in Russian form. 0% adoption.
85: Wikipedia/ESA/NASA use 'Sergei Korolev'. Born in Zhytomyr, spoke Ukrainian natively. A Russian city is named after him. VOA confirmed Moscow actively diminishes his Ukrainian heritage. Most complete identity erasure in dataset.
86: Wikipedia/Britannica use 'Bohdan Khmelnytsky' (Ukrainian form). Success story for historical figures.
87: Wikipedia/MoMA/Tate use 'Kazimir Malevich'. Art world's catalog records, exhibition histories, auction databases make this immovable.
89: Wikipedia actually uses 'Sviatohirsk Lavra' (Ukrainian) — contradicts 0% computational adoption. Wikipedia editors adopted it but broader corpus has not.
